characters.physical_details: Brown bushy hair, usually tied back for work and coming loose by evening. Average height. Left-handed with quill or pencil, wand in her right hand. The word MUDBLOOD remains carved in silver-white scar tissue on her left forearm, usually hidden by glamour and sleeves until chapter 12.

key_relationships.draco_malfoy: Former enemy turned research partner and romantic counterpart; she calls him Malfoy until the private first-name shift in chapter 18.
key_relationships.ron_weasley: Ex-husband; amicable divorce, still attentive to her habits.
key_relationships.narcissa_malfoy: Cautious mutual respect; Narcissa controls key information about the Manor.

characters.occupation_status: Department of Mysteries researcher on magical theory and ambient-field decay; based at Malfoy Manor for the archive assignment.
characters.literacy_languages: English; reads Latin, Ancient Greek, runes, and can work through archaic anchoring notation.

state_transitions.change: Begins sustained Mark examinations and first direct physical contact with Draco.
Mark resonance begins; she can feel Draco's location and pain at distance.
Finds the binding folio and keeps its contents from Draco.
Leaves the Manor after the rupture.
Returns, agrees to no more private managing of facts, and shifts to Draco privately.

characters.availability: Present throughout. At the Manor from chapter_02 through chapter_16 except short London trips in chapter_05 and chapter_13; in London full-time in chapter_01 and chapter_17; returns in chapter_18.

characters.physical_details: Pale, angular, grey-eyed, and thinner than he was at school. Left-handed. The Dark Mark on his left forearm began waking again eight months before the story; he looks half-slept and underfed.

key_relationships.hermione_granger: Former enemy turned research partner and romantic counterpart; he calls her Granger until the private first-name shift in chapter 18.
key_relationships.narcissa_malfoy: Mother; protective, withholding, and the family authority on the Manor.
key_relationships.lucius_malfoy: Father in Azkaban; source of inheritance, damage, and household legacy.

characters.occupation_status: No formal employment; lives on Malfoy wealth, manages the Manor and family archive, and rarely leaves home.
characters.literacy_languages: English; reads Latin, French, and Malfoy family shorthand/property notation, but needs Hermione for runic and structural theory.

state_transitions.change: Lets Hermione into the Manor and agrees to the examination arrangement.
Recognizes the house is answering Hermione in ways it never answered him.
The Mark spills the memory of taking it at sixteen into Hermione.
Sees Hermione's scar and their dynamic changes permanently.
Admits to himself that he loves her.
Finds the binding folio and tells Hermione to leave.
Learns from Narcissa that the house cannot fabricate the connection, agrees to explicit honesty rules, and shifts to Hermione privately.

characters.availability: Present throughout and based at the Manor for the full novel.

characters.physical_details: Elegant and tightly composed, with blonde hair streaked grey. Her hands show age more clearly than her face. She moves through the Manor like someone who learned its moods long ago.

key_relationships.draco_malfoy: Son; the center of her honesty and protectiveness.
key_relationships.hermione_granger: Cautious respect; she judges Hermione worth telling partial truths to.
key_relationships.lucius_malfoy: Husband in Azkaban; real marriage, but never one the house deeply answered.

characters.occupation_status: No formal occupation. Lives apart from the Manor in a rented house in Knightsbridge and remains in unresolved postwar legal limbo, neither pardoned nor convicted.
characters.literacy_languages: English, French, and Latin; familiar with the Manor's old notation and behavior from decades inside it.

state_transitions.change: Visits, recognizes the Manor's new activity, and withholds explanation.
Tells Hermione the house creates conditions but withholds the non-fabrication limit.
Tells Draco the house amplifies but cannot fabricate, and that Lucius never resonated with it.

characters.availability: Appears in chapter_04, chapter_07, and chapter_18.

characters.physical_details: Scar, glasses, untidy hair, and a more settled adult build. Tired in the way overworked adults are, not in the way wartime children were.

key_relationships.hermione_granger: Best friend; has earned the right to say the hard true thing.
key_relationships.ginny_potter: Wife; stable marriage under public pressure.

characters.occupation_status: Auror at DMLE with his own office; married to Ginny and father of James.
characters.availability: Appears in chapter_05, chapter_13, and chapter_17.

characters.physical_details: Tall, broad, red-haired, and freckled; sturdier than he was at school, partly from running.
characters.occupation_status: Works with George at Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes and manages the books for the second shop.

characters.physical_details: Sharp-featured, well-dressed, and socially controlled; presentation is part of how he survives post-war suspicion.

key_relationships.draco_malfoy: Closest friend; knows how isolated Draco has become.

characters.occupation_status: Does consulting work and is trying to secure a Diagon Alley storefront under the Nott name.

timeline.story_start: Late October 2005 in London, as charm failures, Floo outages, and ambient-field decay become impossible to ignore.
timeline.estimated_span: Roughly six and a half weeks, from Hermione's first arrival at the Manor to her return after a seven-day separation.

seasonal_track.season: Late October into early November: damp London cold, first November air at the Manor.
November into late November: frost begins, daylight shortens, and the Manor grows warmer as the bond deepens.
December: hard frost, early dark, public crisis conditions, one week of separation, then return.

key_events.event: Croaker authorizes the Malfoy archive assignment
The paired archive appears and proximity becomes procedural
Tiberius's ledger reveals a twelve-point anchor network and the decay's structural cause
Hermione finds the binding folio and conceals it from Draco

key_events.timing: About five and a half weeks after arrival

key_events.event: Narcissa confirms the house cannot fabricate connection; Hermione returns after seven days

geography.primary_setting: Malfoy Manor in Wiltshire, a sentient estate built into Britain's anchoring infrastructure; London scenes center on Hermione's flat, the Ministry, and neutral meeting places away from shared history.

key_locations.name: Main Library
key_locations.details: The Manor's largest working room and the center of the novel. Centuries of Malfoy acquisition sit here, plus the visible research tables, family records, and access point to the deeper archive.

key_locations.name: Gated Archive
key_locations.details: A hidden archive nested inside the library. It initially opens and holds only when Hermione and Draco are both present; by chapter 16 it can hold Draco alone briefly, but only unstably. It contains the binding folio, Tiberius's ledger, and the oldest anchoring records.

key_locations.name: Drawing Room
key_locations.details: The war-trauma room: chandelier, marble, preserved cold, and Bellatrix's violence. The house forces it open in chapter 9 and Draco and Hermione re-enter it together in chapter 18.

key_locations.name: Servants' Kitchen
key_locations.details: The oldest kitchen still used as a kitchen. Breakfasts, late tea, domestic routines, and Theo's visit happen here.

key_locations.name: Guest Wing
key_locations.details: Hermione's rooms. The house repeatedly alters the guest-wing route to and from the library to manage proximity.

key_locations.name: Hermione's Kentish Town Flat
key_locations.details: A 1,400-square-foot flat above a shuttered dry cleaner, chosen after the divorce for predictability, fixed walls, and no surprises.

key_locations.name: Narcissa's Knightsbridge House
key_locations.details: A rented London house separate from the Manor, used because she no longer lives full-time at the estate.

key_locations.name: British Anchor Network
key_locations.details: At least twelve ancient anchor sites and filum threads across southern and central England; known points include Salisbury Plain, Glastonbury, the Welsh marches, London near the Ministry, Godric's Hollow, the Severn valley, and the Devon coast.

distances: London and the Manor are normally trivial by Apparition or Floo, but the Floo becomes unreliable; the Mark resonance still reaches London weakly.
Inside the Manor, route lengths vary with the house's state; the guest-wing/library walk can be welcomingly short or lengthen from twenty-three to forty-one paces.
The house repeatedly draws the guest wing, library, and shared working rooms into closer relation when it wants Hermione and Draco near each other.

world_rules: Britain's ambient magical field is collapsing gradually; failures show up in Floo travel, healing, concealments, household enchantments, and children's accidental magic.
The decay is structural: ancient anchor points and filum threads were damaged across both wars and left unrepaired.
Malfoy Manor is itself one anchor house in that larger infrastructure, not just a residence built on top of it.
The Manor reads inhabitants, changes temperature and layout, and opens or withholds rooms according to local relational conditions.
Its bonding architecture can amplify an existing attachment, widen proximity/body-state awareness, and reward concordance; it cannot fabricate a connection from nothing.
The hidden archive and related rooms are threshold-sensitive: paired presence is the normal condition, though later the archive can hold Draco alone only briefly and unstably.
Sustained direct contact between Hermione and Draco through the Mark quiets the curse and deepens the resonance.
Dark-magic wounds do not heal cleanly; glamours can hide the scar until heavy magic or sustained strain destabilizes them.
Apparition remains usable; the Floo network becomes increasingly unreliable and can fail outright for hours.

power_structure.mechanism: She can read the theory, build diagnostics, and translate the ambient-field crisis into actionable models.

power_structure.over: Physical access to the Manor and family archive
power_structure.mechanism: It is his house and his family's collection, even when the house itself complicates that ownership.
The house controls layout, temperature, thresholds, and access to key research rooms.

power_structure.over: Interpretation of the Manor's history and limits
power_structure.mechanism: She knows what the house did in her own marriage and withholds or releases that knowledge strategically.

power_structure.over: Public response to the magical decay
power_structure.mechanism: Institutional control of crisis messaging, infrastructure access, and emergency policy.

power_structure.over: The relationship terms from chapter_11 to chapter_16
power_structure.mechanism: She knows the binding folio's contents before Draco does and keeps that asymmetry in place.

objects.name: The Dark Mark
objects.origin: Voldemort's curse brand, given in July when Draco was sixteen, with Lucius holding him in place.
objects.status: Reactivated eight months before the novel; quiets under Hermione's direct contact, worsens again during separation, and keeps a resonance link to her.

objects.name: The Mudblood scar
objects.origin: Bellatrix Lestrange's cursed knife in the Malfoy Manor drawing room during the war.
objects.status: Permanent silver-white lettering on Hermione's left forearm; usually glamoured and sleeved, then openly seen by Draco in chapter 12.

objects.name: The Binding Folio
objects.origin: An old family manual on the Manor's bonding architecture and its maintenance for the Malfoy line.
objects.status: Found by Hermione in chapter 11 and concealed; found by Draco in chapter 16. Its key activation clause is water-damaged, leaving the house's limit ambiguous until Narcissa clarifies it in chapter 18.

objects.name: Tiberius's Ledger
objects.origin: Fourteenth-century field notes with later Abraxas glosses.
objects.status: Maps at least twelve anchor points and filum threads, establishing the decay as structural rather than random.

objects.name: The Levett Manuscript
objects.origin: A fourteenth-century construction and maintenance text for anchoring infrastructure.
objects.status: Introduces the plaiting/rethreading model and knot-points needed for repair work.

income_sources.source: Ministry room-and-board stipend for the Manor assignment
income_sources.reliability: Temporary while the assignment is active

knowledge_state.knows: The Mark resonance lets her feel Draco's location and pain at distance.
The house responds to Hermione with warmth, shortened routes, surfaced books, and opened access that it does not give him.
The binding folio says the Manor amplifies existing attachment, widens paired awareness, and works best when the pair are ignorant of the mechanism.
The house cannot fabricate connection and Lucius never resonated with it the way Draco does.
The Manor's bonding behavior has precedent in her own early marriage, but not at the same depth as Draco and Hermione's.
Hermione found the binding folio weeks earlier and kept its implications from him.
Narcissa confirms the house amplified but did not fabricate what existed between him and Hermione.
Hermione is living and working at Malfoy Manor; by chapter 13 he also knows the rumors are public.
Hermione is spending most of her time at the Manor for research and worries the house will narrow her world around itself.

environmental_constants: Tea is the default domestic gesture: offered, made, and shared in both comfort and confrontation.
The library smells of old paper, dust, and mineral preservation charms.
The Manor's warmth and cold are responsive; after the rupture it lapses toward ordinary winter cold and ordinary architecture.
Stone corridors, moving distances, and altered echoes are the house's recurring physical language.
The novel moves through English late autumn into hard December: frost, early dark, and flat winter light.
Lavender preservation scent recurs in towels, linens, and prepared rooms.
The Mark's pain is a restless ache or burn from dead command channels, and the resonance is bodily spillover rather than telepathy.
